title: Anthropic Model Ban Reshapes AI Sovereignty Debate as G7 Grapples With Trusted Access
subhead: The US export control order on Anthropic's frontier models and the G7's emerging 'trusted partners' framework put AI sovereignty at the center of this week's diplomacy, while China tightens indium controls and the UK moves to ban social media for under-16s.

sections.heading: Anthropic Model Ban Exposes New Frontier in Export Controls
sections.body:
On June 12, the US government issued an export control directive ordering Anthropic to suspend access to its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all non-US persons, citing national security concerns tied to the models' cybersecurity capabilities and an alleged jailbreak vulnerability. Anthropic had roughly 90 minutes to comply. It responded by shutting down both models worldwide — even for its own employees — because it could not technically separate foreign nationals from its global user base in that window.

According to Bloomberg News and Outlook Business, some early testers in Anthropic's Glasswing programme — including Cisco and Dragos — retained access to Mythos Preview, while EU cybersecurity agency Enisa, which had been invited to join Glasswing, was informed it would no longer receive access. Anthropic had limited Mythos Preview to about 200 organizations after the model identified thousands of software vulnerabilities. The company argued the government's concerns stemmed from a 'narrow and disputed' jailbreak scenario.

MEP Brando Benifei described the shutdown as proof that 'tech sovereignty was never abstract.' French President Emmanuel Macron called the restrictions 'strictly nationalist' while acknowledging US security concerns as legitimate, and said he expected Washington to broaden Mythos access within weeks. The episode has already prompted European allies to accelerate calls for domestic AI alternatives, according to reporting by MSN and TRT World.
sections.whyItMatters: The Anthropic order marks the first time export controls — historically reserved for physical weapons and dual-use hardware — have been applied at this scale to a commercial AI model. The 90-minute compliance window and global shutdown reveal how model-level access can be weaponized as a geopolitical tool, and how fragile allied access to frontier AI has become.

sections.heading: G7 Floats 'Trusted Partners' Framework for AI Access
sections.body:
At this week's G7 summit in France, leaders discussed a 'trusted partners' scheme that would grant select allied nations and companies special access to advanced US AI models — including those affected by the Anthropic order — while keeping them out of reach for others. The proposal effectively creates a diplomatic security clearance for frontier AI, according to three diplomatic sources cited in headlines and Memeburn's reporting from the summit.

The concept was discussed at a closed-door lunch attended by the CEOs of OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google, alongside G7 heads of state. Anthropic's Dario Amodei pushed for structured access to frontier models and chip trade agreements excluding China. OpenAI's Sam Altman called for 'an international forum for discussion that establishes globally accepted standards for testing.' Google's Demis Hassabis also advocated for a US-led coalition on global AI rules.

Macron's pushback on the Anthropic shutdown was direct: he warned that 'nobody will buy US AI if they fear it can be switched off overnight', according to Memeburn. The tension between Washington's security instincts and allied governments' demand for predictable access remains unresolved.
sections.whyItMatters: The trusted partners concept would formalize a two-tier AI world — allied access versus restricted access — but Macron's objection underscores the credibility problem: if the US can pull the plug in 90 minutes, no agreement on access can fully reassure sovereign buyers.

sections.heading: China Tightens Indium Phosphide Controls, Squeezing AI Data Center Supply Chains
sections.body:
China is stepping up customs scrutiny on indium and indium phosphide (InP) exports, requesting more detailed information about shipments and end users, according to Reuters. China controls roughly 70% of global indium production, and InP has no ready substitute for the high-speed optical chips that connect GPUs inside AI data centers.

InP wafer prices have surged approximately 250% — from about $1,400 to $5,000 per six-inch wafer — since Beijing imposed export controls on the compound in early 2025. Nvidia-backed chipmaker Coherent warned of a shortage earlier this year, and AXT, the world's second-largest InP substrate producer, described the export permits as its most significant challenge. Coherent's CEO reportedly raised the licensing delays directly with Chinese counterparts during a US business delegation visit.

The TNW analysis notes this fits a familiar pattern: where Washington restricts China's access to advanced chips and chipmaking tools, Beijing answers by leveraging its dominance over critical materials. InP targets the optical layer rather than the logic layer — a constraint that does not stop any single chip from working, but slows how fast entire data centers can be wired together.
sections.whyItMatters: China's indium squeeze is not a ban — it is slow permitting as leverage. The 250% price spike in InP wafers directly translates into higher costs and longer build times for AI infrastructure globally. It also signals that Beijing will match every US chip restriction with a materials countermeasure.

sections.heading: France vs. US: Digital Tax Collision at the G7
sections.body:
French President Emmanuel Macron refused to scrap France's 3% digital services tax on US tech giants, telling reporters that 'the US does not decide European law' just hours before meeting Trump at the G7 summit. Trump responded by threatening 100% tariffs on French wines, according to Reuters and MSN.

The French digital tax, which primarily targets American companies including Google, Amazon, and Meta, has been a recurring irritant in transatlantic trade relations. Macron's defiance at the G7 transforms a bilateral tax dispute into a sovereignty question: whether European governments can set their own digital fiscal policy without facing trade retaliation from Washington.

The confrontation over the digital tax intersects with the AI access dispute. Both issues force the same question — whether US technology dominance gives Washington effective veto power over how allied governments regulate and tax that technology within their own borders.
sections.whyItMatters: France's refusal to drop the digital tax and Trump's tariff threat create a direct collision between tech sovereignty and trade policy. If the US retaliates with 100% wine tariffs, it sets a precedent that any country taxing American platforms faces economic punishment — making the digital tax question a template for how far tech-dependent allies can push back.

sections.heading: UK Bans Social Media for Under-16s, Adds AI Chatbot Restrictions
sections.body:
The UK government confirmed a ban on social media platforms — including TikTok, Snapchat, and Instagram — for users under 16, effective by spring 2027. Platforms will be required to verify users' ages to keep minors out. Nine in ten respondents to a national consultation backed a minimum age of 16.

The UK also became the first country to ban 'romantic' AI chatbots for under-18s, requiring age verification before any sexually explicit AI features can be accessed. However, mainstream chatbots like ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini are not subject to the outright ban — only their 'intimate functionalities' must be age-gated. The Bureau of Investigative Journalism notes this leaves a significant gap: general companion apps and smaller AI platforms that enable emotional dependency or intimate interactions are not covered by the romantic chatbot ban.

When Character.AI barred minors in October 2025, a crop of smaller developers quickly set up to capture displaced teenagers, advertising on Reddit and Discord — suggesting the ban may redirect rather than eliminate risky AI interactions.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK is pioneering the most aggressive Western regulatory framework for both social media and AI chatbot access for minors. But the gap between banning platforms and covering the long tail of AI companion apps means the practical effect may be displacement rather than protection — a pattern other countries watching the UK experiment will need to reckon with.

sections.heading: China Develops Low-Cost Stealth Material, Raising Mass-Production Questions
sections.body:
Researchers at Foshan University in China reported a new microwave-absorbing composite made from expanded graphite and titanium dioxide — cheap, readily available materials — published in the peer-reviewed Journal of Ceramics in April and reported by SCMP this week. The material combines strong electromagnetic wave attenuation with a simple manufacturing process, potentially reducing production costs compared to existing stealth coatings that rely on expensive graphene and carbon nanotubes.

SCMP also reported debate over whether commercial 'stealth paint' is already available in China, raising questions about whether military-grade radar-absorbing technology is being sold cheaply as ordinary house paint. While the claims remain unverified, the broader development is clear: Chinese researchers are systematically working to make stealth coatings cheaper and more scalable.
sections.whyItMatters: If stealth coatings can be mass-produced at the cost of ordinary paint, the strategic cost advantage of stealth technology — currently limited to expensive, carefully maintained military platforms — erodes significantly. A world where stealth is cheap and commoditized changes the calculus for air defense systems globally.

sections.heading: Social Media Overtakes Traditional News for First Time, Reuters Institute Finds
sections.body:
The 2026 Reuters Institute Digital News Report, based on survey data from over 85,000 respondents across 48 regions, found that social media platforms have overtaken TV and news websites as the primary news source globally for the first time. TikTok and Instagram are rising in influence as news distribution channels, while X (formerly Twitter) continues to decline as a news platform since Elon Musk's acquisition.

The report also documented a significant rise in AI chatbots as news sources, with weekly use growing across all age groups — a trend that raises concerns given the rate of AI hallucinations and factually incorrect responses. In India, 58% of respondents get news from YouTube and 56% via WhatsApp, according to Newslaundry's analysis of the same data.

Online creators are increasingly replacing traditional journalists as primary news sources in many regions, driven by social algorithms optimized for engagement rather than accuracy.
sections.whyItMatters: The shift to social media as the dominant news source — combined with growing reliance on AI chatbots for information — creates a structural vulnerability for information integrity. When algorithmic discovery and creator-driven content replace editorial gatekeeping, the incentives favor engagement over accuracy, and the infrastructure for misinformation scales automatically.

bottomLine: This week's defining development is the US government's use of export controls to shut down frontier AI models — not chips, not equipment, but the models themselves. The Anthropic order, the G7's trusted partners response, and Macron's pushback together establish that AI access is now a sovereign issue, not a market question. Meanwhile, China matched Washington's chip restrictions with an indium phosphide squeeze that is already inflating AI data center costs by 250%, proving that the tech sovereignty contest runs in both directions. The France-US digital tax clash and the UK's social media ban for minors add regulatory dimension: governments are asserting sovereign authority over digital platforms — but with very different tools, and very uneven results.
sources: Outlook Business, Reuters, Bloomberg News, Memeburn, TRT World, SCMP, TNW, CryptoBriefing, MSN, CNN, The Bureau of Investigative Journalism, Reuters Institute, Newslaundry, Yahoo News/Social Media Today, Washington Post
